brief:
The single most striking feature of this week's data is the absolute stillness across the entire yield curve — not a single basis point has moved at any tenor — which, paradoxically, is itself the most important signal. When a curve freezes in the face of **9.13% CPI**, a repo rate at **10%**, a deeply troubled fiscal position with revenue collection at just **44.9% of target**, and a 10-year bond that devolved **10%** barely five weeks ago, the market is telling us something uncomfortable: participants are in a collective wait-and-see crouch, unwilling to price in either a hawkish pivot or an easing narrative. The front end remains anchored near policy — the 91-day at **9.85%** sits just 15 basis points below repo, and call money at **9.8%** confirms overnight liquidity is adequate but not loose. The 182-day at **10.20%** trades above repo, which is rational, but the 364-day at **10.07%** sits 13 basis points *below* the 182-day, creating a visible kink that signals demand concentration in the one-year bucket, likely driven by banks warehousing SLR-eligible paper at a tenor that flatters both liquidity ratios and mark-to-market exposure. Further out, the curve resumes its upward slope to the 5-year at **10.82%** and the 10-year at **11.15%**, but then we encounter a genuinely distorted long end: the 2-year at **11.95%** trades **80 basis points** wide of the 10-year and **113 basis points** above the 5-year, while the 15-year at **12.68%** leaps above the 20-year at **11.65%** by over a hundred basis points. These are not normal term premium dynamics — they are artifacts of illiquidity, sporadic issuance, and stale pricing in tenors where secondary market depth is virtually nonexistent.

The structural backdrop explains why the market is frozen but fragile. Excess liquidity at **৳1.9 lakh crore** sounds abundant, but it is unevenly distributed and increasingly parked in short-duration government paper rather than flowing into private credit — the ADR at **75.8%** confirms banks remain well below any lending constraint, and M2 growth of **9.5%** is running below nominal GDP, suggesting monetary conditions are, in practice, tighter than headline liquidity implies. The government's fiscal predicament deserves close attention: with only **44.9%** of revenue collected and ADP implementation limping at **11.5%**, the fourth quarter of FY26 will inevitably bring a borrowing surge. Total debt at **৳21.5 lakh crore** — **40% of GDP** — is manageable by emerging-market standards, but the interest-to-revenue ratio at **22%** is the number that should keep this committee awake. Every 50-basis-point rise in the weighted average cost of new issuance translates into meaningful fiscal pressure. The 10-year auction's **1.4x bid-cover** with **10% devolvement** on March 8 was a quiet warning: the market absorbed the 5-year comfortably at **1.6x** with zero devolvement, but balked at duration. When the government returns to market for its fiscal year-end funding push, that reluctance at the long end will be tested — and if BB is forced to devolve more, it is effectively monetizing the deficit, adding fuel to an inflation fire that food prices at **9.3%** are already stoking.

For portfolio positioning this week, the call is relatively clear: stay short, stay liquid, and be opportunistic at the belly only on weakness. The 91-day at **9.85%** and the 364-day at **10.07%** offer near-risk-free carry within the policy corridor with strong bid-covers of **1.92x** and **1.75x** respectively — these are the tenors to accumulate. The 5-year at **10.82%** is the only duration point I would consider adding on a tactical basis, given its clean auction history and zero devolvement, but only if we see a concession toward **11%** in secondary. Avoid the 10-year until we see bid-cover recover above **1.6x** and devolvement drop to zero — paying **11.15%** for a bond that devolved at auction is not compensation, it is a trap if the government floods this tenor in Q4. The anomalous 2-year at **11.95%** and 15-year at **12.68%** should be treated as mispriced illiquidity, not value. The key catalyst over the next two weeks is the government's updated borrowing calendar for the final quarter: if net T-bill and BGTB issuance targets rise materially — as fiscal arithmetic virtually guarantees — expect the long end to reprice **20-30 basis points** higher, and expect BB to face uncomfortable questions about whether the corridor ceiling at **11.5%** can hold. If Brent pushes past **$80** or the taka weakens beyond **122**, the inflation narrative hardens and any hope of near-term easing evaporates entirely. Stay defensive, preserve optionality, and let the market come to us.
